Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to put in around 34 hours a week, but with limited resources at home and working on the side, I couldn't always stick to a plan. I'd often study late at night when the house was quiet, listening to lectures and audio materials since that's how I learn best. Some weeks I was more disciplined, other weeks everything just fell apart. I kept telling myself I'd get more organized, but it never quite happened the way I wanted.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram everything into a few intense days. Other times, when nothing felt urgent, I'd let things slide and focus on other responsibilities. Being 29 and having bills to pay means school isn't always the top priority, even when I know it should be. I wish I had more consistency, but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually I'd log into the LMS when I knew something was due soon or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd go in just to check if I'd missed anything important, especially after being away for a few days. The gamified tasks they added were kind of interesting at first, so those badges and points would pull me in occasionally. But honestly, most of the time it was deadline panic that got me to log in.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a real routine for me. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in daily, but that didn't last. The gamification stuff helped a bit because earning points felt like progress, but my motivation was too low to keep that momentum going. When stress piled up from other parts of my life, logging in felt like just another burden rather than something helpful.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd often play them while doing other things or during my commute. The discussion posts were okay too because hearing other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. I appreciated when instructors would summarize key points in audio format. Reading long texts on screen was harder for me, so anything auditory was a lifesaver.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With my auditory learning style, sitting down to read dense text just didn't work well for me, especially when I was already tired. I also skipped some of the optional practice quizzes because they felt time-consuming and I was already stretched thin. The peer interaction features were there but I didn't engage much because I felt disconnected from the other students.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exam or if it helped me understand something I was confused about. The gamified elements made some tasks feel more rewarding in the moment, like I was actually accomplishing something. But honestly, with my low energy and motivation this semester, most things had to feel absolutely essential for me to prioritize them. If it seemed like busy work, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. I'd look at them and think, 'I should do this,' but then the required stuff would pile up and the optional things would get pushed aside. Required activities had that weight of consequences behind them, so I'd force myself through even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford with everything else going on. It's sad because some of those optional materials probably would have helped me understand things better.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download audio lectures and listen to them on my phone when I was out or working. I took handwritten notes that I'd review later, and I used some free YouTube videos to supplement what we were learning. With limited internet at times, downloading things and studying offline was often more practical for me. The LMS wouldn't know about any of that effort.
Q10: There were plenty of times when I was genuinely studying but the LMS had no idea. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times while doing chores or on the bus. I'd discuss concepts with a friend who was also taking the course, working through problems together at his place. I spent hours reviewing my handwritten notes and trying to connect ideas in my head. All of that learning was invisible to the system.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a video once, download it if I could, and then watch it multiple times offline. Same with any audio content or PDFs I could save. My internet isn't always reliable, so working offline was often necessary. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have engaged with it five or six times after that. It was just practical given my situation.
Q12: Things got really hard around midterms when my work schedule picked up and I was dealing with some personal stuff that had me feeling pretty down. The stress was overwhelming and my resilience just wasn't there. I'd sit down to study and my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about bills or family issues. The sadness made everything feel heavier, like even simple tasks required enormous effort. I fell behind and never really caught up.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was a big one because I was working and studying and not sleeping enough. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I had to make choices, and sometimes school lost out to immediate needs like work shifts. The confusion from falling behind made it worse because I didn't know where to start, so I'd just avoid it altogether.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't retained anything because I was too stressed or tired. Sometimes I'd click through things just to see the completion checkmarks without really engaging. It felt like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was the sadness that made everything feel pointless sometimes. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. By the time I finally started, it was often too late to do a good job, which just made me feel worse about myself.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline looming, I'd let things drift. When something was due soon, I'd suddenly find the energy to log in and cram. But that pattern meant I was always rushing and never really learning deeply. The LMS probably shows spikes of activity right before due dates, and that's accurate. It wasn't healthy but it was how I survived.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it gave me a small boost of motivation. But when feedback was critical or when I got low grades, it honestly made me want to disengage more. With my low resilience, negative feedback felt crushing rather than constructive. I'd avoid checking my grades sometimes because I didn't want to face the disappointment.
Q18: The course structure was okay but it didn't really accommodate my situation well. Having five online courses at once was overwhelming, and the way content was released sometimes felt relentless. The gamified elements were a nice touch and did help a bit with motivation. But overall, the structure assumed I had more time and energy than I actually did. I needed more flexibility than the course provided.
Q19: The gamification made some things easier because it broke tasks into smaller pieces with rewards. That helped when my motivation was low. But the sheer volume of content and the expectation of regular participation was hard for someone in my situation. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes. Having more audio-based content would have helped me a lot given how I learn best.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's trying to engage but struggling to actually produce work. You might log in, watch videos, read through materials, but then hit a wall when it comes to assignments because of stress, confusion, or just running out of time. The intention is there, the effort to understand is there, but the output doesn't happen. It's frustrating because the activity shows you're trying, but the results don't reflect that.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Someone might be doing most of their learning offline, like I did with downloaded materials and personal notes. Or they might be naturally quick learners who don't need as much LMS time to understand concepts. The LMS only sees what happens on its platform, but learning happens everywhere. A student could be deeply engaged with the material without leaving much of a digital footprint.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and whether you submitted things on time. That's useful for tracking surface-level activity. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you actually understood what you watched or read. They miss offline studying, emotional struggles, and life circumstances that affect performance. They see the clicks but not the person behind them.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials, it's wrestling with ideas and trying to connect them to what you already know. For me, real engagement happened in those quiet moments when I was listening to lectures and things finally clicked. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting just to stay afloat this semester. It wouldn't show the sadness that made getting out of bed difficult some days, or the stress from financial pressures. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the conversations with friends about course content. It wouldn't show that I actually wanted to succeed but was drowning in circumstances beyond my control. The data sees a failing student, but it doesn't see the full picture of why.
